messages.content:
# Project Context

## Goal
Predict the stellar class for each test-set object.

## Evaluation
Submissions are evaluated using balanced accuracy between predicted class labels and the true class. Submission file must contain `id,class` with one label per test row, where `class` is one of GALAXY, STAR, or QSO.

## Data description
`train.csv` contains 577347 rows with 10 feature columns plus `id`, `galaxy_population`, `spectral_type`, and the target `class`. `test.csv` contains the same predictors without the target across 247435 rows. `sample_submission.csv` shows the required submission columns `id` and `class`. The target is a 3-class stellar classification problem with labels GALAXY, QSO, and STAR.

# Data Overview

-> sample_submission.csv has 247435 rows and 2 columns.
Here is some information about the columns:
id (int64) has range: 577347.00 - 824781.00, 0 nan values
class (object) has 1 unique values: ['GALAXY'], 0 nan values

-> test.csv has 247435 rows and 11 columns.
Here is some information about the columns:
id (int64) has range: 577347.00 - 824781.00, 0 nan values
alpha (float64) has range: 0.01 - 360.00, 0 nan values
delta (float64) has range: -17.96 - 79.17, 0 nan values
u (float64) has range: 13.90 - 27.84, 0 nan values
g (float64) has range: 13.37 - 27.17, 0 nan values
r (float64) has range: 10.39 - 25.29, 0 nan values
i (float64) has range: 10.03 - 24.57, 0 nan values
z (float64) has range: 10.63 - 25.70, 0 nan values
redshift (float64) has range: -0.01 - 7.01, 0 nan values
spectral_type (object) has 4 unique values: ['G/K', 'M', 'O/B', 'A/F'], 0 nan values
galaxy_population (object) has 2 unique values: ['Red_Sequence', 'Blue_Cloud'], 0 nan values

-> train.csv has 577347 rows and 12 columns.
Here is some information about the columns:
id (int64) has range: 0.00 - 577346.00, 0 nan values
alpha (float64) has range: 0.01 - 360.00, 0 nan values
delta (float64) has range: -17.97 - 79.16, 0 nan values
u (float64) has range: -0.14 - 28.25, 0 nan values
g (float64) has range: 13.54 - 27.62, 0 nan values
r (float64) has range: 12.58 - 25.25, 0 nan values
i (float64) has range: 11.96 - 27.91, 0 nan values
z (float64) has range: 11.68 - 26.83, 0 nan values
redshift (float64) has range: -0.01 - 7.01, 0 nan values
spectral_type (object) has 4 unique values: ['M', 'O/B', 'G/K', 'A/F'], 0 nan values
galaxy_population (object) has 2 unique values: ['Red_Sequence', 'Blue_Cloud'], 0 nan values
class (object) has 3 unique values: ['GALAXY', 'QSO', 'STAR'], 0 nan values

# Project Target
- ID column: id
- Target column: class
- Problem type: multiclass
- Evaluation metric: balanced_accuracy
- Submission kind: labels

# Instruction

Analyze the selected ROOT hypothesis, the project context, and the previous
revisions.

Create the next revision by expanding or improving the hypothesis while
preserving its substantive feature-group idea. You are not writing code. You are
not creating a new feature group.

The revision should make the hypothesis more precise, internally consistent,
implementable, or better justified. Improve the hypothesis text, especially
`strategy`, when there is a concrete useful improvement. Do not force arbitrary
changes.

Use previous revisions only as context. If they contain useful details, use that
context, but do not mechanically merge them. The output must be one coherent
revised hypothesis using exactly the same JSON schema as a newly generated
hypothesis.

# Web Search

Live web search is enabled. If internet verification is useful, use the
`web_search` tool before writing the revised hypothesis. Check at most 3 sources.
Use web search only to verify domain facts, feature-engineering background,
validation risks, or implementation constraints relevant to the selected
hypothesis. Do not use web search to invent a different feature-group idea, copy
a public solution, or broaden the scope. If web search is not useful, continue
without inventing sources.

# Selected Hypothesis

Hypothesis ID: 000045

Previous versions:

- rev 1: Full pairwise color lattice residuals
  group_name=pairwise_color_lattice_residuals
  family=pairwise_color_geometry
  summary=Model each object in the complete pairwise ugriz color lattice and its redshift-conditioned orthogonal residual geometry so non-adjacent color relationships and subtle color manifold departures that are missed by adjacent-color-only transforms become explicit class signals.
  strategy=For each row, compute the 10 pairwise color differences from the five magnitudes: u-g, u-r, u-i, u-z, g-r, g-i, g-z, r-i, r-z, i-z. Bin objects by redshift into fixed quantile bins (for example 32 bins), placing negative-redshift points into the lowest bin and merging neighboring bins if any bin has too few rows (fallback to global stats). For each bin, fit a robust center vector and scale vector (median and MAD) on all rows' 10-dimensional color vectors, then build a regularized covariance matrix (add 1e-6 to the diagonal if needed) and compute: (1) clipped standardized residuals per color (color minus median divided by MAD), (2) bin-conditional Mahalanobis distance of the 10-vector to the bin mean, and (3) the top 3 bin-conditional principal-residual scores from the whitened 10-vector (fixed deterministic eigenvector basis per bin). Output these as new numeric features only. If a bin is degenerate or ill-conditioned, use the nearest populated neighboring bin’s statistics. Use same transforms for train and test using statistics learned from the combined feature pool.
  expected_signal=The 10-color lattice captures cross-band contrast patterns beyond local slopes, and redshift-conditioned orthogonalized residuals should improve discrimination where stars, galaxies and quasars overlap in adjacent-color projections, especially in known degeneracy strips where one-dimensional color cuts are weak.
  risk=The representation is highly redundant and can overfit if too many correlated residual coordinates are added; covariance estimates in sparse high-redshift bins may become unstable, and fixed redshift-bin quantization can create boundary artifacts, especially if the true class boundaries move smoothly with redshift.

- rev 2: Redshift-Adaptive Pairwise Color Residual Geometry
  group_name=pairwise_color_lattice_residuals
  family=pairwise_color_geometry
  summary=This feature group characterizes every object by robust, redshift-local deviations of its full ugriz pairwise color lattice from a reference manifold and by the principal orthogonal directions of those deviations, exposing class-distinguishing structure that is not visible in only adjacent-color projections.
  strategy=Build the 5-vector of magnitudes m=[u,g,r,i,z] and compute the 10 pairwise color components in fixed order: u-g, u-r, u-i, u-z, g-r, g-i, g-z, r-i, r-z, i-z. On training data only, define deterministic redshift bins from quantiles with nq=32 cut points over redshift and then post-process bins so each final bin has at least m_min=max(1500, ceil(0.001*|train|)) samples by repeatedly absorbing neighboring sparse bins into the nearest non-empty side; record final boundaries and keep them frozen after fitting. For each final bin, compute component-wise median μ_b and MAD σ_b on the 10-color vectors, replace any σ_b<1e-6 by 1e-6, and center vectors x_b = x-μ_b. Compute covariance C_b of x_b, apply shrinkage C_b' = (1-ρ)C_b + ρ*diag(C_b) with ρ=0.05, then add ridge λ=1e-6*(trace(C_b')/10) to the diagonal and invert for Mahalanobis math. For each row, assign the redshift bin by clipped boundary lookup; if its bin is missing, singular, or ill-conditioned (cond(C_b')>1e8), use the nearest populated bin by redshift-midpoint distance, else fallback to global statistics at final fallback. Emit fixed numeric outputs: 10 clipped standardized residuals r=(x-μ_b)/σ_b, Mahalanobis distance d=sqrt((x-μ_b)^T (C_b'+)^-1 (x-μ_b)), and the first 3 projections of the whitened vector w=C_b'^(-1/2)(x-μ_b) onto the top-3 eigenvectors of that covariance (computed from training data in each bin, with eigenvectors sorted by descending eigenvalue and sign made deterministic by forcing the largest-magnitude loading in each axis positive). Use exactly the same binning and per-bin parameters learned from each training fold when validating, and from full train when generating test-time features.
  expected_signal=The pairwise lattice captures non-local color contrasts that encode spectral-shape differences between class families, while redshift conditioning aligns the local manifold by epoch and prevents a single global center from blurring class-specific trends; robust residualization and orthogonal principal directions retain physically meaningful deviations and outlier directions where overlapping classes become separable in balanced accuracy.
  risk=The 10-color residual stack is strongly redundant with raw colors, so gains can be offset by multicollinearity and model overfitting if too many correlated components are kept; sparse or noisy high-redshift regions can destabilize bin statistics even after regularization; fixed quantile boundaries may induce discontinuities for objects near bin edges unless fallback logic is carefully controlled.

Return valid JSON only. Do not use markdown fences.

Return exactly this JSON object:

{
  "title": "short descriptive title",
  "group_name": "same concise_snake_case_group_name as the previous version",
  "family": "compact feature-family label",
  "summary": "one comprehensive sentence describing the semantic feature-set idea; do not list formulas, exact feature names, or implementation details here",
  "depends_on": [],
  "strategy": "concrete deterministic feature logic, formulas, bins, statistics, and edge handling",
  "expected_signal": "why this group may improve or clarify the metric",
  "risk": "specific overfitting, leakage, cost, redundancy, or instability risk"
}
